queue.name: ID10072: Hamiltonian and Equations of Motion: (Dq)/(Dt)

master_row.title: Hamiltonian and Equations of Motion: (Dq)/(Dt)
master_row.canonical_statement: \begin{theorem}[Hamilton’s Equations] Let \(X_H\) be the Hamiltonian vector field defined by \(\iota_{X_H}\omega = -\mathrm{d}H\). Then the evolution of any observable \(F\) is given by \[ \frac{\mathrm{d}F}{\mathrm{d}t} = \{F,H\}_{\omega}. \] In coordinates, the equations of motion for \((q,p)\) are \[ \frac{\mathrm{d}q}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p},\qquad \frac{\mathrm{d}p}{\mathrm{d}t} = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q} + F_{\text{ext}}. \] \end{theorem}
master_row.formation_note: Recovered from a D:\All source document under ‘Hamiltonian and Equations of Motion’.
master_row.dictionary: Hamiltonian and Equations of Motion: (Dq)/(Dt) — (dq)/(dt) = (∂ H)/(∂ p), (dp)/(dt) = -(∂ H)/(∂ q) + F_ext.
master_row.encyclopedia: Hamiltonian and Equations of Motion: (Dq)/(Dt) is a variational / legendre derivative, written (dq)/(dt) = (∂ H)/(∂ p), (dp)/(dt) = -(∂ H)/(∂ q) + F_ext.. It arises from a variational or Legendre procedure, defining conjugate quantities or stationarity conditions. It is built from ∂, H, p, q, F_, defining (dq)/(dt). Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.
